name: Echo (Go)
what_it_does: Minimalist high-performance web framework for Go. Echo emphasizes extensibility and built-in features: automatic TLS via Let's Encrypt, WebSocket support, HTTP/2, graceful shutdown, and a rich middleware ecosystem. Competes with Gin as a top Go web framework. Known for cleaner API design and better built-in features than Gin while maintaining comparable performance.
use_cases: Build Go-based agent API services with Echo's built-in JWT middleware, CORS, and rate limiting
Create WebSocket endpoints for real-time agent communication using Echo's built-in WebSocket support
Deploy agent APIs with automatic TLS certificate management via Echo's Let's Encrypt integration
Build agent microservices with automatic request logging, recovery, and request ID middleware
Implement agent HTTP/2 servers for performance-sensitive agent communication endpoints
not_for: Python or JavaScript ecosystems — Echo is Go-only
Ultra-simple services — Go's net/http stdlib is sufficient without a framework
Projects requiring Gin's specific ecosystem — many Gin plugins don't work with Echo
best_when: You're building a Go agent backend and want built-in JWT middleware, WebSocket support, and automatic TLS without additional libraries.
avoid_when: You're using Gin (ecosystem differences make switching costly), or building non-Go backends.

auth.notes: Built-in JWT middleware, KeyAuth middleware, and BasicAuth middleware available as echo/middleware package.

performance.rate_limits: No rate limits — runs on your infrastructure

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: Echo's HTTPError type includes code and message. Binder errors include field-level validation context. Custom error handler via e.HTTPErrorHandler for centralized error response formatting.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: HTTP handler idempotency is application responsibility.
known_agent_gotchas: echo.Context is not safe for use after the handler returns — don't store context in goroutines spawned during the request without acquiring a copy
echo.Bind() is opinionated about struct tags — uses json, form, query, param tags; missing tags means fields are not bound from the respective source
Middleware order matters — middleware added via e.Use() runs in reverse order of handler middleware; outer layers run first on request, last on response
Built-in JWT middleware from echo/middleware has different options than golang-jwt — verify the version compatibility
Graceful shutdown requires signal handling setup manually — Echo doesn't add OS signal handlers by default
echo.Context.Bind() tries all sources — can cause confusion when query param and JSON body have same field name; use more specific binding methods

security_score_components.security_notes: Auto-TLS via Let's Encrypt built in. Built-in JWT and CSRF middleware. Go's type system prevents injection vulnerabilities.
